coredata.dc:title: Highly selective and sensitive determination of doxycycline integrating enrichment with thermosensitive magnetic molecular imprinting nanomaterial and carbon dots based fluorescence probe

coredata.dc:description: Doxycycline (DOX), a typical tetracycline antibiotic, is widely used because of its excellent antibacterial activity. To develop effective method for DOX has attracted much more attention. Herein, a new detection technology integrating magnetic solid phase extraction (MSPE) based on thermosensitive magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers (T-MMIPs) and fluorescence spectrometry based on carbon dots (CDs) was established. Thermosensitive magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers (T-MMIPs) was designed for selective enrichment of trace DOX. The synthesized T-MMIPs showed excellent selectivity for DOX. The adsorption performance of T-MMIPs varied with temperature in different solvents, which could achieve the enrichment and rapid desorption of DOX. In addition, the synthesized CDs had stable fluorescent property and better water-solubility, and the fluorescence of CDs was significantly quenched by DOX due to the internal filtration effect (IFE). Under the optimized conditions, the method resulted in good linearity over the range from 0.5 to 30 μg L−1, and the limit of detection was 0.2 μg L−1. The constructed detection technology was validated with real water samples, and excellent spiked recoveries from 92.5 % to 105.2 % were achieved. These data clearly indicated that the proposed technology was rapid, highly selective, environmentally friendly, and possessed significant potential application and development prospects.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of 10 mg L−1 CDs under different excitation wavelengths. (B) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs; (C) Stability of CDs fluorescence. (D) Fluorescence stability of CDs in 20 days. (E) UV–visible absorption spectrum of CDs. (F) Comparison of UV–vis absorption spectrum of DOX (curve a), excitation spectrum of CDs (curve b) and emission spectrum of CDs (curve c); (G) Fluorescent lifetime of CDs.

Optimization of MSPE parameters. (A) Selection of eluent; (B) Effect of elution volume; (C) Effect of elution time; (D) Effect of adsorbent dosage; (E) Effect of adsorption time; (F) Effect of pH; (G) Effect of adsorption temperature; (H) Effect of elution temperature; (I) Effect of ionic strength; (J) Effect of sample volume. Initial conditions of MSPE: eluent, 10 mL ethanol; elution time, 9 min; dosage of T-MMIPs, 50 mg; adsorption time, 1 h; pH, pH 6.5; adsorption temperature, 25 °C; elution temperature, 25 °C; no NaCl added; sample volume, 50 mL. Each factor was optimized while the other factors were maintained unchanged, and the optimal value was used in the subsequent optimization process.

Optimization of fluorescence detection conditions. (A) Concentration optimization of CDs; (B) Optimization of pH.

Comparison with other fluorescence methods developed in recent years for detecting DOX.
Spiked recoveries of DOX from three actual water samples with the constructed sensor.

head.ce:title: Highly selective and sensitive determination of doxycycline integrating enrichment with thermosensitive magnetic molecular imprinting nanomaterial and carbon dots based fluorescence probe

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Doxycycline (DOX), a typical tetracycline antibiotic, is widely used because of its excellent antibacterial activity. To develop effective method for DOX has attracted much more attention. Herein, a new detection technology integrating magnetic solid phase extraction (MSPE) based on thermosensitive magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers (T-MMIPs) and fluorescence spectrometry based on carbon dots (CDs) was established. Thermosensitive magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers (T-MMIPs) was designed for selective enrichment of trace DOX. The synthesized T-MMIPs showed excellent selectivity for DOX. The adsorption performance of T-MMIPs varied with temperature in different solvents, which could achieve the enrichment and rapid desorption of DOX. In addition, the synthesized CDs had stable fluorescent property and better water-solubility, and the fluorescence of CDs was significantly quenched by DOX due to the internal filtration effect (IFE). Under the optimized conditions, the method resulted in good linearity over the range from 0.5 to 30 μg L−1, and the limit of detection was 0.2 μg L−1. The constructed detection technology was validated with real water samples, and excellent spiked recoveries from 92.5 % to 105.2 % were achieved. These data clearly indicated that the proposed technology was rapid, highly selective, environmentally friendly, and possessed significant potential application and development prospects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, antibiotics have been widely utilized in various fields such as in animal husbandry and aquaculture due to their effective anti-microbial and anti-infective abilities (Fan et al., 2022; Lan et al., 2017). However, antibiotics are often easily available without a prescription, which results in the misuse and abuse of antibiotics. In addition, antibiotics are not easily degraded in the environment, leading to their residue in the environment and food (Wang et al., 2022a), and which has received high attention from regulators and public (Liu and Wong, 2013). Remarkably, reports showed that antibiotics posed a potential threat to human health such as bacterial resistance, liver damage, and impact on tooth development (Lee et al., 2016; Meng et al., 2019). Doxycycline (DOX) is a tetracycline antibiotic with excellent efficacy and has excellent anti-infective ability against gram-positive and negative bacteria (Sysay and Wirat, 2017). At the same time, DOX was very cheap, which makes it widely used in animal husbandry (Ghaemi and Absalan, 2015). Once people eat the foods with residual doxycycline, the ingested DOX can cause liver damage, allergic reactions, and gastrointestinal disease. The European Union has announced a maximum residue limit of 100 μg kg−1 for doxycycline in poultry muscle (Zhuang et al., 2021). While the maximum residue limits for doxycycline in fish stipulated by China and Japan are 100 and 50 μg kg−1, respectively (Xu et al., 2020). Therefore, it is urgent to establish sensitive and rapid methods for monitoring DOX.

At present, many methods have been developed for analyzing, including electrochemical technologies (Cui et al., 2020; Gashu et al., 2022; Annalakshmi et al., 2019; Yao et al., 2019), high performance liquid chromatography (Dil et al., 2020; Li et al., 2022a; Ghasemi et al., 2022), immunoassay (Ashuo et al., 2020), colorimetric method (Wang et al., 2019), spectroscopy (Wang et al., 2021), etc. Some chromatographic methods usually require expensive equipment and longer analytical time, and rely on very professional operators (Liu et al., 2018). Electrochemical methods have the advantages of high sensitivity, fast response time and low cost, however there are some limitations in their application. For example, the coexistence of substances from unknown “pollutants” can seriously interfere with detection; expensive materials may be required to fabricate the working electrode; lengthy operational procedure for fabricating electrodes is required to overcome selectivity problems and maximize sensitivity (Li et al., 2021b; Chen et al., 2018; Sahragard et al., 2022). Immunoassays also results in the advantages of strong specificity, high sensitivity, and large detection scale. But they usually require a complex synthesis and purification process (Zhang et al., 2021). Fluorescence spectroscopy (FL), a spectroscopic technique, has attracted more and more attention in recent years because of its simple operation, fast detection, and good selectivity. In recent years, carbon dots (CDs), a newly fluorescent nanomaterial, emerged as a twinkling star and achieved wide attention in fluorescence detection due to their excellent fluorescence properties (Zeng et al., 2022; Yan et al., 2022; Barrientos et al., 2023). There have been many reports on the detection of antibiotics by designing CDs as fluorescent probes (Wang et al., 2022a, 2022b, 2022c; Tang et al., 2022; Tian and Fan, 2021). Thara et al. obtained nitrogen-doped carbon points (N-CDs) with blue fluorescence by simple microwave assisted pyrolysis using urea and glucose as raw materials. The synthesized N-CDs has strong fluorescence intensity, stability, water solubility and biocompatibility. Tetracycline significantly reduced the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs due to the inner filtration effect (IFE). Based on this principle, they developed a fluorescence sensor for specifically detecting tetracycline with linear range of 0 μM - 1 μM and detection limit of 56 nM (Thara et al., 2023). Zhang et al. synthesized poplar flower CDs (pf - CDs) with excellent luminescent properties and good biocompatibility using poplar flowers as the precursor. The experimental results demonstrated that enfloxacin (ENR) could enhance the fluorescence intensity of pf-CDs, and the mechanism was different from that mentioned above, and might be due to the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bond between pf-CDs and enfloxacin (ENR) leading to the occurrence of aggregation induced emission. The fluorescence intensity of pf-CDs was positively correlated with ENR concentration in the range of 0.2–100 μM, and the detection limit was low to 0.076 μM. The obtained pf-CDs has been successfully applied to the detection of ENR in dairy products and domestic water (Zhang et al., 2023). However, actual water samples usually contain complex substrates, and very low concentrations of target analytes, which may bring challenges to fluorescence detection. Therefore, it is necessary to pretreat the samples before fluorescence detection properly.

The pretreatment technologies used for extracting antibiotics included liquid-liquid extraction (Mohebi et al., 2020), dispersed solid phase extraction (Lu et al., 2020), liquid-liquid micro-extraction (Ma and Row, 2021), solid phase microextraction (Balakrishnan et al., 2006), magnetic solid phase extraction (MSPE) (Liang et al., 2019). The MSPE is a new pretreatment technology derived from solid phase extraction, which has been extensively employed in the extraction and separation of trace contaminants in environmental samples (Speltini et al., 2016; Meira et al., 2021). The MSPE retains the advantages of solid phase extraction, and the magnetic adsorbent is reusable and easy to separate using a magnet. The selection of magnetic adsorbent is very crucial in the MSPE process. Fe3O4 has few functional groups on its surface, which leads to its poor adsorption capacity. For gaining good extraction performance, functional modification is inevitable. Recently, there have been many materials that can be used for functional modification. Among them, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are often employed due to their high selectivity according to the known “lock and key” mechanism (BelBruno, 2019). As an excellent nanomaterial, MIPs has apparent advantages such as durability and low cost. The functionalization of molecular imprinting often involves coating or laminating the surface of Fe3O4 with silica, and further surface modification can be obtained by fixing hydrophilic ligands, hydrophobic ligands, or ion exchange groups, which make the modified magnetic nanomaterials selectively enrich different target compounds (Yu et al., 2022). The thermosensitive polymer material is a compelling intelligent polymer material whose solubility decreases with the increase of temperature, and a specific temperature will result in phase separation and turbidity. When the temperature drops, it becomes clear and transparent solution (Qin et al., 2011a; Li et al., 2018). So far, there have been no literatures on the selective detection of DOX by combining thermosensitive magnetic molecular imprinting and CDs.

In the present work, thermosensitive magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers (T-MMIPs) are designed and prepared with thermosensitive functional monomers. The T-MMIPs will not only retain the characteristics of easy separation, but also have thermosensitive properties for extraction. The enrichment and rapid desorption of the target can be realized by utilizing the difference of its solubility in different solvents with the change of temperature. Magnetic solid phase extraction is developed as an enrichment step before fluorescence detection with CDs. Carbon dots can specifically recognize DOX, and T-MMIPs can significantly improve the selectivity and sensitivity of the method. The two materials were systematically characterized, and the adsorption capacity of T-MMIPs was further evaluated. The significant factors were also investigated in detail. The adsorption kinetics and adsorption isotherm were also studied. In addition, the proposed method was also used to analyze actual water samples to verify the reliability of the method.

Doxycycline hydrochloride was purchased from Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glyoxylic acid, urea, N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM), 2,2-azobisisobutyronitrile, ferric chloride, sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate, sodium acetate, 3-aminopropyltriethoxy silane (APTES), trimethylolpropane triacrylate (TMPTA), n-hexane, tetracycline, ciprofloxacin, chlortetracycline hydrochloride, enrofloxacin, and oxytetracycline hydrochloride were achieved from Aladdin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other reagents used and the instruments for characterization (Li et al., 2022b) were shown in supporting information, and all chemicals utilized were of analytical grade without further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout.

Carbon dots were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. The glyoxylic acid (0.3 g) and urea (0.15 g) were mixed completely in 10 mL ultrapure water, which was put into a PTFE autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 12 h. The crude product was filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane. The obtained supernatant was purified with a dialysis membrane (molecular weight 1000 Da) for 24 h to remove larger molecules. Finally, the purified solution was dried in an oven for 1 day to prepare CDs powder.

The method for synthesizing Fe3O4 was as follows: sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate (0.8 g) was stirred at room temperature in 80 mL ethylene glycol until a transparent solution was formed. Ferric chloride (2.7 g) and sodium acetate (4.65 g) were then added to the mixture, which was then shifted to a PTFE autoclave and thoroughly stirred and maintained reacting at 180 °C for 12 h. The final products were cleaned several times using methanol and distilled water and dried in vacuum at 40 °C for 24 h.

The synthesis of Fe3O4@SiO2 drew on our previous research with significant modifications (Li et al., 2021a). Fe3O4@SiO2 was synthesized as follows: 0.6 g of Fe3O4 particles were dispersed in a mixture containing methanol (100 mL) and ultrapure water (20 mL) and sonicated for 15 min. Then 25 % ammonia (10 mL) and 4 mL APTES were fortified into the solution, which was reacted by stirring at 25 °C for 12 h and then passed through N2 for 10 min, then heated to 60 °C and kept mechanically stirring for 1 h. After naturally cooled to room temperature, the obtained solids were washed 3 times with methanol and dried in vacuum (40 °C) for 24 h.

The synthesis of T-MMIPs was based on previous research (Shi et al., 2019), and the synthesis method was as follows: doxycycline hydrochloride (0.1 g) was dissolved in acetonitrile (100 mL), and NIPAM (0.5 g) was added as the functional monomer. The solution was sonicated for 30 min to dissolve thoroughly and then maintained 24 h at room temperature for pre-polymerization. Fe3O4@SiO2 (0.6 g) was dissolved in the pre-polymerization solution. Then TMPTA (2 g) as the crosslinking agent and AIBN (0.05 g) as the initiator were fortified into the solution, which was sonicated for 30 min, and then stirred at 60 °C for 24 h. The gained materials were washed with methanol until no DOX was detected in the washing solution. The material was dried in vacuum at 40 °C for 24 h.

T-MNIPs were synthesized with consistent steps with the T-MMIPs synthesis except that DOX was not added in the pre-polymerization stage. T-MNIPs was used for comparison in the subsequent performance evaluation of the proposed T-MMIPs method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Magnetic solid phase extraction and fluorescence detection
ce:section.ce:para: T-MMIPs (60 mg) were fortified into the sample solution (50 mL) spiking DOX at 20 μg L−1. NaCl was not added. The mixed solution was shaken at 30 °C and 200 rpm for 40 min, allowing DOX to be adsorbed to the surface of T-MMIPs. The adsorbents were separated by an external magnetic field. Then 8 mL of ethanol was added (3 times, 4 min each) at 25 °C to elute DOX from the adsorbents. The eluate was combined and dried with the N2 stream, and the residue was reconstituted with 50 μL of ethanol and 940 μL (pH 11) buffer solution. Then 10 μL CDs solution was added to obtain the concentration of 10 mg L−1 for fluorescence detection. After incubation for 4 min, the fluorescent response of the solution was gained with excitation wavelength at 310 nm and emission wavelength at 400 nm.

Both adsorption kinetics and isotherm experiments were performed under the optimal experimental conditions. In the adsorption kinetics experiments, 60 mg T-MMIPs were added to DOX in aqueous solution (50 mL) at a concentration of 20 μg L−1 and the recoveries of DOX were determined in different intervals (10–120 min). In isothermal adsorption experiments, 60 mg T-MMIPs were added to DOX sample solutions (50 mL) with concentrations in the range of 0.5–150 μg L−1, and the recoveries of DOX were determined and the adsorption amounts were calculated. The equations of adsorption kinetics and isotherm models were provided in supporting information.

In order to verify the practicability of the established method, three water samples were collected from Changping Park (Beijing, China), South Lake (Nanjing, China), and Meizi Lake (Yunnan, China). The water samples were filtered through 0.22 μm membrane and stored at 4 °C. The spiked sample solutions at 1, 4 and 20 μg L−1 were prepared with actual water samples.

Fig. 1A exhibits the TEM image of the as-prepared CDs, which indicated that CDs have a nearly spherical structure with a particle size of 9.33 nm. Fig. 1B shows the TEM image of Fe3O4 and it is obviously that Fe3O4 has spherical structure. Fig. 1C clearly shows that T-MMIPs have a transparent film on their surface, and their particle size increases significantly, which can be attributed to the emerged imprinted layer. Fig. 1D exhibits the infrared spectrum of CDs. The absorption peak at 3100 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching and bending vibrations of OH and NH. The absorption peak at 1620 cm−1 may be caused by CO vibration. The absorption at 1400 cm−1 can be interpreted as CN vibration (Wang et al., 2022b). Fig. 1D shows the existence of hydroxyl and amino groups on CDs, making it highly soluble and stable in water (Zhang et al., 2023). Fig. 1E exhibits the XRD patterns of Fe3O4 and T-MMIPs prepared. As shown in Fig. 1E, the characteristic featured diffractions of T-MMIPs appeared at 29.2°, 35.4°, 42.7°, 53°, 56.9°, and 62.4°, which were consistent with the characteristic diffraction peaks of Fe3O4, indicating that the synthesis of T-MMIPs did not alter the lattice structure of Fe3O4 (Xiong et al., 2022). In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 1F), the peak of 580 cm−1 was observed as a characteristic FeO peak (Liu et al., 2022). In the infrared spectrum of T-MMIPs, the absorptions at 1080 cm−1 and 990 cm−1 were contributed to Si-O-Si and Si-O-Fe absorption (Tong et al., 2023). The results showed that the synthesis of Fe3O4 and T-MMIPs was successful.

Fig. 2A exhibits the fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. The results showed that CDs exhibited non-excitation dependence, so the fluorescence of CDs may be caused by surface state emission (Song et al., 2015). Fig. S1 shows the 3D contour photo of CDs. With the excitation wavelength at 310 nm, the fluorescence intensity of CDs reached the maximum value. Therefore, 310 nm was used as the excitation wavelength in the subsequent experiments.

Generally, CDs will have different fluorescent properties at various pH value. In this experiment, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was measured while the pH of CDs solution was adjusted at pH 1–14. As shown in Fig. 2B, the fluorescence intensity of CDs varied at different pH, and the fluorescent response was strongest at pH 11. In addition, the fluorescent stability of CDs was also examined (Fig. 2C and D). The fluorescent intensity of CDs declined slightly within 4 min and then kept stable after 4 min. There was no apparent difference in the fluorescence intensity of CDs within 20 days, indicating that CDs had excellent fluorescence stability.

In general, the reported quenching mechanisms between the target analyte and quenching agent include static quenching, dynamic quenching, photoproduced electron transfer (PET), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), and inner filter effect (IFE) (Iqbal et al., 2016), etc. Fig. 2E shows that the mixing of CDs and DOX do not alter the absorption spectrum of CDs and proves that the mechanism of fluorescence quenching is not static quenching. In addition, it can be seen from Fig. 2F that the excitation spectrum of CDs partially overlapped the UV–visible absorption spectrum of DOX. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs may be due to FRET or IFE (Zu et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2021). Fluorescence lifetimes are used to distinguish between IFE and FRET (Zu et al., 2017). FRET showed a change in fluorescence lifetime when quenching agents were added, whereas, for IFE, fluorescence lifetime was almost unaffected. The fluorescence lifetimes of CDs and CDs + DOX were measured to be 7.27 and 7.15 ns, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2G, the difference is minimal, indicating that the addition of DOX does not change the fluorescence lifetime of CDs. Therefore, fluorescence quenching should be induced by IFE.

The choice of the eluent has a significant influence on the desorption of the target analytes during the MSPE process and also indirectly affects the subsequent fluorescence analysis (Zhou et al., 2021a). In order to achieve excellent desorption performance, common solvents including methanol, ethanol, acetonitrile, acetone, and n-hexane were tested as the eluents. Fig. 3A shows that methanol and ethanol resulted in much better extraction efficiencies than acetone and acetonitrile. The extraction efficiency of n-hexane was the worst. This phenomenon may be due to the fact that DOX is a polar compound, while methanol and ethanol are polar solvents. According to the “like dissolves like” principle, methanol, and ethanol achieved better elution performance. The polarity of acetone is weaker than that of methanol and ethanol, and hexane is a non-polar solvent, which is not conducive to the dispersion of T-MMIPs, thus affecting the elution efficiency of DOX. The extraction efficiency of DOX was the highest with ethanol as the eluent. Therefore, ethanol was optioned for elution in following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of eluent volume and elution time
ce:section.ce:para: In the MSPE process, elution volume and elution time are also crucial parameters (Zhou et al., 2021b; He et al., 2019), which will determine the elution performance and further affect the sensitivity of the developed method. Herein, a variety of experiments were designed to investigate the influence of these parameters in the range of 2–10 mL and 4–20 min, respectively. As exhibited in Fig. 3B, the extraction efficiency of DOX raised with the increscent eluent volume over range of 2–8 mL. However, the extraction efficiency of DOX remained unchanged when the elution was greater than 8 mL. This indicated that the adsorption and desorption rates of DOX between the eluent and the adsorbent might reach an equilibrium. In order to avoid the waste of solvent, 8 mL was optioned for subsequent experiments. Fig. 3C shows that the extraction efficiency of DOX gradually raised with the extension of elution time. After 12 min, the extraction efficiency of DOX maintained almost unchanged with the extension of elution time, indicating that the adsorption reached an equilibrium. As a result, 12 min was utilized as the optimal elution time.

Adsorbent dosage and adsorption time are key factors affecting the adsorption performance in the MSPE process (Tong et al., 2022). In this study, the dosage of adsorbent (T-MMIPs) was investigated, ranging from 30 to 90 mg. From Fig. 3D, it was apparent that the recovery of DOX raised when the amount of adsorbent was increased from 30 to 60 mg. This can be attributed to increased available active sites (Gaho et al., 2022). With a further increase in adsorbent usage, the recovery remained almost unchanged. This was because the adsorption equilibrium of DOX between the surface of the adsorbent and the aqueous phase was reached (Lu et al., 2019). In the MSPE process, sufficient adsorption time is essential for the target analytes to reach adsorption equilibrium on the adsorbent surface. The impact of adsorption time was investigated from 20 to 100 min (Fig. 3E). It was found that the extraction efficiency of DOX reached the maximum with the adsorption time at 40 min. The recovery of DOX was basically stable when the adsorption time was over 40 min. When the adsorption reached equilibrium, the adsorption rate of DOX on the T-MMIPs surface was equal to its desorption rate from T-MMIPs surface. Therefore, extending the adsorption time could not improve the adsorption efficiency. In order to achieve complete adsorption of DOX and save material and time, 60 mg T-MMIPs and 40 min were employed as the optimal adsorbent dosage and adsorption time, respectively.

The charge of DOX changes with pH, which affects the interaction with the adsorbent due to the presence of hydroxyl and amine groups in the DOX molecule (Ma et al., 2022). Therefore, solution pH is a critical factor in the MSPE process. The effect of pH on the enrichment of DOX was studied over range of pH 3–11 (Fig. 3F). The results showed that the enrichment performance reached the best value at pH 5. The extraction efficiencies of DOX at other pH values were lower than that at pH 5. The reason may be that the π - π interaction between adsorbent and DOX is inhibited as pH increases (Gao et al., 2012). Besides, the pKa of DOX is 3.0 (Xu et al., 2021), indicating that it is easily destroyed in alkaline aqueous solutions, which results in a low recovery rate under alkaline conditions. Therefore, pH 5 was selected as the optimal pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of adsorption temperature and elution temperature
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that poly-NIPAM is a temperature-sensitive material that can change its structure and size depending on the temperature of the environment (Zhang et al., 2014; Li et al., 2018). When the temperature is below the low critical solution temperature (LCST) of the material, it dissolves because intermolecular hydrogen bonds dominate. However, when the solution temperature is higher than the LCST, the hydrophilicity of the material will be decreased. The obtained T-MMIPs were promising candidate showing such temperature-sensitive properties, and so the temperature may have significant effect on the enrichment of DOX in the MSPE process. In the MSPE procedure, the temperature was examined over the range of 20–45 °C. As seen in Fig. 3G, the extraction efficiency of DOX on T-MMIPs exhibited conspicuous temperature dependence. It was obvious that the extraction efficiency of DOX reached the highest at 30 °C. This may be because the volume and shape of T-MMIPs imprinting cavity also change during the process of external temperature change (Qin et al., 2011b). This was because when the aqueous solution temperature was lower than 30 °C, N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM) of T-MMIPs would expand, which was conducive to DOX entering the blotting chamber and conducive to the adsorption of DOX molecules, resulting in the increase of adsorption capacity. When the temperature exceeded 30 °C, NIPAM contracted significantly, and the imprinting cavity collapsed, which was not conducive to DOX entering the imprinting cavity and resulted in a decrease in extraction efficiency. In addition, the temperature also affects the elution performance during the elution process. As shown in Fig. 3H, the recovery of DOX gradually increased with the rise in elution temperature. When the elution temperature continued to increase after 25 °C, the recovery of DOX decreased significantly. This indicated that when the elution temperature was 25 °C, the volume of the T-MMIPs imprinting chamber reached the maximum, and the desorption efficiency of DOX reached the highest. Therefore, 30 °C was used as the adsorption temperature, and 25 °C was employed as the elution temperature in subsequent experiments.

The ionic strength may affect the adsorption performance (Liu et al., 2017). This may be due to the fact that the concentration change of salt in the sample solution will change the solubility of DOX as well as its diffusion rate in water due to the salting-out effect, salting-in effect, and viscosity change. The effect of ionic strength was investigated by adding NaCl ranging from 0 to 20 % (w/v). The recovery of DOX was highest when the NaCl concentration was 0 % (w/v) (Fig. 3I). The recovery of DOX decreased with the increase of NaCl concentration, which may be due to the salting-in effect, which promoted the dissolution of DOX in water and thus reduced its recovery. However, the decrease was very slight. Therefore, no NaCl was added in subsequent experiments.

Sample volume is a significant parameter for evaluating MSPE performance. As shown in Fig. 3J, the highest recovery of DOX was obtained when the sample volume was up to 50 mL. Then the recovery of DOX gradually decreased with the enlargement of sample volume, which may be contributed to the fact that the increased DOX could not be adsorbed by a fixed amount of adsorbent or could not be eluted by a certain amount of eluent. A sample solution of 50 mL was used in subsequent experiments to ensure a high extraction efficiency and enrichment factor.

Kinetic studies can help to understand the time-dependence of the targets in the adsorption process (Wang et al., 2012), and the kinetic experiments were carried out by varying the adsorption time in the range of 10–120 min. It was apparent that the amount of the adsorbed DOX increased rapidly from 10 to 40 min, which meant that the imprinted caves were filled quickly by DOX molecules. Generally, pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order adsorption kinetic models are the main often-used models for studying adsorption kinetics (Debnath and Das, 2023). In present study, these two models were also exploited to study the adsorption kinetics of DOX. The experimental results were shown in Fig. 4A and B and the relevant parameters were shown in Table 1 . The adsorption kinetic fitted results showed that the correlation coefficient of the pseudo-second-order kinetic model was higher than that of the pseudo-first-order kinetic model. This indicated that the adsorption of DOX followed the pseudo-second-order kinetic model, and the adsorption of DOX was chemical adsorption which may be the rate-controlling factor.

The adsorption isotherm can help to deeply understand the adsorption mechanism. In order to investigate the adsorption of DOX on T-MMIPs, the adsorption characteristics under optimal adsorption conditions were investigated and the adsorption mechanism was estimated using Langmuir, Freundlich and Temkin isotherm models. The adsorption isotherms of DOX on T-MMIPs were shown in Fig. 4 and the parameters were shown in Table 2 . The results showed that Langmuir model was suitable for simulating the adsorption of DOX, and the adsorption of DOX tended to monolayered adsorption (Debnath and Das, 2023). According to the Langmuir model, the maximum adsorption amount of DOX was 80.28 μg L−1.

As shown in Fig. 5 , DOX significantly reduced the fluorescent intensity of CDs. In the fluorescence detection process, the CDs concentration and pH of the solution were crucial parameters and optimized to obtain effective detection parameters. The difference of fluorescence intensity (F0 F) (ΔF) was closely tied up to DOX concentration and used as a reference signal. F0 and F are the fluorescent intensity of CDs without DOX and with DOX, respectively.

Fig. 5A demonstrates that ΔF increased significantly with the rise of CDs concentration and reached the maximum value with CDs concentration at 10 μg mL−1. With the continuous increase of CDs concentration, the ΔF value decreased gradually. And that is because when the CDs concentration was too low, there were not enough CDs to fully interact with DOX. However, if the concentration of CDs was too high, the phenomenon of self-imbibition may occur (Liu et al., 2017), which will affect the fluorescence intensity. Therefore, 10 μg mL−1 CDs concentration was optioned as the optimal concentration.

Due to the rich carboxyl and amino groups in synthesized CDs, pH may have a significant impact on the interaction between CDs and DOX (Seidi et al., 2020; Tian and Fan, 2021). Fig. 5B shows the impact of pH on the fluorescent detection of DOX with CDs. When pH was less than pH 11, DOX had a weak quenching effect on CDs fluorescence intensity. When pH was more than pH 12, ΔF was significantly decreased. Therefore, after comprehensive consideration, pH 11 was selected as the best detection condition.

The features of the developed method were evaluated under optimal conditions. As exhibited in Fig. 6 , ΔF showed excellent linearity within the concentration range of 0.5–30 μg L−1 (1.13–67.57 nM). The limit of detection (LOD) of DOX was calculated to be 0.2 μg L−1 (0.45 nM) (LOD = 3σ/s, where σ was the standard deviation of the blank signals (n = 15), and s was the slope of the linear curve). In order to verify the reliability and stability of the method, its intraday and inter day precisions and stability were examined. In the intraday precision experiment, the established MSPE-FL technique was used to detect water samples with DOX concentrations of 1 μg L−1, 4 μg L−1, and 20 μg L−1 under optimal experimental conditions. In the interday precision experiments, DOX water samples at the above three concentration levels were measured once a day for six consecutive days. The data obtained indicated that the intraday and interday precisions for DOX at concentrations 1 μg L−1, 4 μg L−1, and 20 μg L−1 were 3.00 %, 2.62 %, 1.10 %, and 4.37 %, 3.67 %, 1.85 %, respectively. These experimental results strongly demonstrated that the method has good reliability and stability.

Generally, other antibiotics in the actual water samples may affect the detection of DOX. The interference effect of different antibiotics on the proposed method was investigated. Fig. 6C shows the selectivity of T-MMIPs and T-MNIPs to target adsorption. The interfering substances were ciprofloxacin (CFX), chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), tetracycline (TC) and enfloxacin (EFX). In the investigation, DOX concentration was kept at 10 μg L−1, and the concentration of interferences was all at 50 μg L−1. The MSPE process and fluorescence detection process were performed as the same as those in Section 2.4. All experiments were carried out under optimal experimental conditions. The poor adsorption of T-MMIPs to other antibiotics was observed, possibly because MMIPs earned specific recognition sites for DOX. The results showed that the proposed method showed excellent selectivity in detecting DOX.

The comparison with other methods developed in recent years based on CDs or molecular imprinting for DOX detection was presented in Table 3 . Wang et al. designed a CDs-based method for DOX specific detection. CDs was synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of phthalic acid and ethylenediamine. DOX and Ca2+ can form a stable fluorescence emission complex with weak fluorescence emission at 511 nm. When DOX was added to CDs solution in the presence of Ca2+, the fluorescence intensity at 511 nm was increased sharply, while the peak value at 390 nm was gradually reduced. This was because CDs could significantly enhance the fluorescence intensity of the complex based on the FRET. At the same time, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased based on IFE. The developed method provided linear ranges of 0.2–2.0 μM and 2.0–10.0 μM, and the LOD was 19.13 nM. CDs could enhance the fluorescence emission of DOX and Ca2+ complexes, which was the characteristic optical property that distinguished DOX from other antibiotics (Wang et al., 2022c). Tang et al. developed a new sensor based on dual-emission fluorescent graphene quantum dots for detecting DOX. The proportional fluorescence probe was designed by grafting Rhodamine B onto F, N co-doped graphene quantum dots (F, N-GQDs). In the presence of DOX, the fluorescence at 466 nm was significantly quenched due to IFE and FRET, while the fluorescence at 592 nm was slightly weakened due to energy transfer in the fluorescence probe emission peak. The sensor exhibited a good linearity in DOX concentration range of 0.04–100 μM, with a LOD of 40 nM (Tang et al., 2022). Compared with other reported fluorescence sensing methods, the proposed method based on MSPE-FL technology has a low detection limit and good selectivity, making it a potential DOX detection method.

Generally, a large number of antibiotics are released from different sources into the aquatic environment, posing a threat to human health. Therefore, it is of crucial significance to establish a sensitive and effective method for detecting trace DOX in the aqueous environment. The proposed method was validated with real water samples. The analytical results were provided in Table 4 . Three water samples were collected for analysis, and no DOX was detected. The recoveries of spiked samples were in the range of 97.3–105.6 %, with precisions lower than 4.2 %. These experimental results indicated that the constructed method possessed good detection performance for DOX in the aqueous solution.

In the present study, T-MMIPs integrating the thermo-sensitivity and magnetism and CDs with strongly stable fluorescence were successfully prepared. Based on MSPE-FL method, high enrichment derived from MSPE with T-MMIPs and sensitive detection with CDs were effectively combined for the determination of trace DOX. It was encouraging to note that T-MMIPs offered significant selectivity over traditional MSPE adsorbents as well as advantages as the same of traditional MSPE adsorbents. In addition, enrichment and rapid desorption can be easily achieved by using the difference of solubility in different solvents with the change of temperature. The selectivity and sensitivity of the method were significantly improved by the combination of T-MMIPs and CDs. The established method based on MSPE-FL was successfully applied to DOX analysis in environmental samples with high spiked recoveries. All these indicated that the constructed method earned broad application prospects in the field of identification and determination of trace antibiotics in the water environment. However, the preparation of specific magnetic molecular imprinting polymers for target antibiotics is very complex, and the enrichment capacity will be raised by reasonably designation of the magnetic molecular imprinting polymers and adjusting the experimental parameters. Meanwhile, other sensitive detection method integrating highly efficient sample pretreatment technique will be a good direction for developing sensitive methods for analysis of antibiotics.
